narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways during the admission review process. The university uses a predictive model to estimate your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it calculated a 55% probability that you would fail—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This decision hinged on just a few key aspects of your background, with your undergraduate GPA, third-year school decile ranking, and family income bracket playing the biggest roles. Your GPA of 2.70, which is below the typical 3.21 average, was the strongest positive force pulling the prediction toward success on the bar exam, as it suggested you have some academic resilience despite not being at the top of the pack. However, your third-year decile of 3—lower than the average of 5.53—pushed the model notably toward predicting failure, indicating your performance in later school years raised concerns about sustaining progress through law school and the bar. Similarly, your upper-middle family income bracket contributed toward the failure prediction, as the model associates it with patterns where students in that range sometimes face unique pressures that impact bar outcomes. Compared to typical students, your LSAT score of 32.50 also trails the average of 36.70, though it wasn't the top driver here, and your full-time status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%. These factors together tipped the scales just over the edge into rejection territory. While this is disappointing, especially given your solid GPA foundation, it doesn't define your potential—many applicants in similar positions have improved through targeted efforts. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, or exploring programs that weigh holistic factors more heavily. Building a stronger narrative around your full-time commitment and any upward trends in your academics could also help in reapplying or appealing to other schools. With focused preparation, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
